Men jag minns också att, fel som SNP: s planer var, var de ännu mer förnuftiga att något som föreslagits av brexiters (därför skulle ett ja-röst ha räddat Skottland från en hård brexit).<br/><br/>Tja, ledighetslägret lärde sig läxan av indyref, så de gick avsiktligt ut ur deras sätt att döda debatten och fokusera på i stort sett mindre frågor, till exempel fisk, böjda bananer eller EU-förordningar. Och de galopperade ut sitt land av mjölk och honung löften, att veta att de återstående lägret (och media) skulle kämpa för att motverka så många lögner berättade under en så kort period.<br/><br/>Det är alltså nödvändigt att ledighet binds genom att ställa rätt fråga och vara tydlig för allmänheten att deras beslut skulle få konsekvenser. Den första frågan om att lämna eller stanna kvar i EU, den andra (och eventuellt den tredje) frågan om den inre marknaden, fri rörlighet, tull osv.<br/><br/>Så till exempel skulle det vara klart (och jag skulle sätta det på valsedeln) att om du röstar ledighet, betyder det att du lämnar, du är inte längre en EU-medborgare, du förlorar all rätt som ger dig (vilket innebär mer pappersarbete nästa gång du försöker resa till EU). Att få slut på den fria rörligheten skulle innebära att ingen inre marknad skulle få tillträde, med negativa konsekvenser för industrin och det skulle automatiskt utlösa en gränsundersökning i Nordirland (tvungen att välja mellan återförening eller stanna kvar i tullunionen) och eventuellt även i Skottland.<br/><br/>Justin Greening föreslår att ha tre alternativ, kvar, en mjuk brexit eller en hård brexit. Jag skulle oroa mig för att dela kvar röst (eller lika lämna omröstning). Så det skulle bara fungera om du hade någon form av system för att eliminera det minst populära alternativet, överför sedan 2: a preferensrösten till den andra.<br/><br/>Men det i sig belyser problemet. Om brexiters ställer frågan kommer de inte att göra något av ovanstående. De vill bara att allmänheten ska skriva på självmordsbrevet och låta dem köra bussen från klippan. De kommer sannolikt att lämna det på två frågor och göra dem "Vill du förbli en slav i Bryssel med hamstrar av invandrare som kommer över för att stjäla dina jobb och ta med knivbrott från rumänska zigenare i hoodies?" eller "vill du se den härliga brittiska imperiet 2.0 stiga medan enhörningens frolic i vetefält, floderna flyter med mjölk och honung, medan pengar regnar från himlen".<br/><br/>Ponzifaktorn<br/><br/>Så med tanke på att vi vet att ledighetslägret ljög (av egen inryckning), att de fuskade och bröt mot reglerna, att de uppenbarligen inte har en aning om vad de gör och effekterna av brexit är redan uppenbara (78% av människor tror att brexit går dåligt), skulle man tro att det skulle vara en slam dunk för kvarlägret. I värsta fall en smal röst för en mjuk brexit, men mer sannolikt än inte en stark röst för kvar.<br/><br/>Som komikern Henning Wehn påpekade, kommer många väljare att rösta lämna igen, helt enkelt för att de genom att rösta kvar skulle erkänna för sig själva och sina kamrater att de hade fel förra gången och deras ego kan bara inte ta den hit. Det är ett fenomen som jag kallar "ponzifaktorn".<br/><br/>En viktig del av någon con är att få märket att känslomässigt begå det (t.ex. genom att låta dem enkelt göra en liten summa pengar). Det är faktiskt intressant att notera hur mycket av brexiters språk är mycket likt vad du skulle höra från en bedragare ("tro på Storbritannien" eller "spara brexit" etc.). Målet här är tvåfaldigt. För det första minskar det chanserna att märket backar ur con när de oundvikliga sprickorna börjar dyka upp. Och för det andra minskar det chanserna att de kör till polisen efter att ha blivit fleece (antingen för att de fortfarande tror på bedrägeriet eller är för generad för att rapportera det).<br/><br/>Charles Ponzi var herre över detta, och vid ett tillfälle hindrade han ett löppass på sitt sällskap genom att helt enkelt gå ut och dela ut smörgåsar och kaffe till dem som väntade på att få sina pengar tillbaka, vilket övertygade många av dem att gå hem. Även vid rättegången vittnade några av märkena som han hade flått till sitt försvar. Många höll fast vid hans aktiebrev och trodde att det bara var en häxjakt mot honom av etablissemanget och att han skulle komma ut ur fängelset, tjäna tillbaka sina pengar och betala dem.<br/><br/>Så jag förväntar mig att minst 40% kommer att rösta lämna i någon form eller form igen.<br/><br/>Upprorskampanjen<br/><br/>Så det ser dystert ut för kvarlägret, brexiters garanteras ett stort block av röster oavsett och de får välja frågan och tidpunkten för omröstningen (och de väljer en tid som är så troligt som möjligt för att leda till en låg sväng av kvarröstare, t.ex. mitt i uni-examen veckor). Det finns dock några faktorer som talar för att de kvarvarande lägren ska stanna, först och främst demografin, eftersom hundratusentals ledighetsväljare har dött, medan det finns många nyregistrerade väljare som nu skulle återstå.<br/><br/>Det finns också vad jag skulle kalla upprorsfaktorn. Många som röstade för att lämna gjorde det av en anledning. De hatade Cameron, det politiska etablissemanget och hans åtstramningspolitik och ville ge honom den tvåfingrade saluten. Men med alla de stora partierna nu sannolikt stöd ledighet (inklusive Corbyn) som vänder saker på huvudet. En hel del av detta omröstning block kan väl skifta för att rösta kvar, bara för att pissa av regeringen.<br/><br/>Det är av dessa skäl jag skulle hävda att en framgångsrik kvarvarande kampanj måste vara en upprorisk anti-etableringskampanj. Dess huvudtema kommer att vara att politikerna inte har en aning om vad de gör, de använder brexit som en politisk fotboll för att främja sina karriärer och driva igenom en agenda som gynnar bara de rika. Samtidigt kommer landet att kruka, järnvägarna är i en röra, NHS är på livsuppehållande och åtstramning driver hela städer i fattigdom. Den enda lösningen är att radera brexit från den politiska agendan så att parlamentet kan tvingas fokusera på viktigare frågor (vilket skulle kräva en stark majoritet för att lösa frågan för en generation).<br/><br/>Som ett resultat skulle jag föreslå att sätta en icke-politiker på plats som "ansikte" av kampanjen. Radio värd James O'Brien eller anti-brexit kampanjare Gina Miller skulle vara bland mina val. Eller kanske en komiker som Marcus Brigstocke eller Al Murray (aka pubägaren). Varför? Eftersom vi försökte vinna på fakta och siffror förra gången, det fungerade inte. Så jag skulle gå för humor och satir för att göra brexiters ser dum.<br/><br/>Och jag tänker inte avstå från att attackera EU självt under kampanjen, det är inte en perfekt institution, det är bara alternativen som är så mycket värre. Sist skulle till exempel Grekland eller vissa EU-institutioners odemokratiska natur peka på att de som var kvar skulle försöka försvara det oförsvarbara. Mitt svar skulle vara ja, men hur exakt ska EU kunna ändra på det?<br/><br/>En framgångsrik kampanj för att stanna kvar kan ha många fördelar, för det första skulle det naturligtvis innebära att Storbritannien stannar kvar i EU. Men jag misstänker att det inte kommer att bli för många champagnekorkar som flyger i Bryssel (sanningen ska sägas, de skulle hellre bli skjutna av Storbritannien!), eftersom de skulle vara medvetna om att få tillbaka ett mer djärvt Storbritannien. Detta skulle kunna tjäna som en föregångare för en verklig förändring av hur EU fungerar.<br/><br/>Jag gissar att de inte skulle överleva en andra folkomröstning, även om de vinner. Oddsen är att partiet kommer att splittras, med centristerna och hårda brexiter som bildar separata partier (varav en fortfarande kommer att kallas det konservativa partiet). Det är möjligt att den hårda brexit-vingen så småningom skulle gå samman med UKIP för att bilda ett högerparti.<br/><br/>Arbetarpartiet kan också splittras. I grund och botten skulle en framgångsrik kvarröstning innebära slutet för Corbyn och hans lilla fraktion av "m<unk>sli brexiters". De skulle sannolikt tvingas ut och så småningom hitta sig in i något hårt vänsterparti. Eller, i vissa fall, särskilt Kate Hoey, gå med i UKIP. Detta skulle faktiskt vara till nytta för arbetarpartiet, det finns gott om möjliga kandidater med en bra chans att bli PM, inklusive flera från vänsterkanten av partiet. Och med Tories i oordning är oddsen för att arbetskraft vinner ett efterföljande val hög.<br/><br/>Å andra sidan skulle en andra ledighet, särskilt en som Corbyn stödde, dela partiet i hälften. De på mitten vänster eller Blairites skulle sluta, eventuellt sammanslagning med centristerna från Tory partiet för att bilda ett nytt centrum mark parti (vilket innebär att en majoritetsregering blir i huvudsak omöjligt).<br/><br/>Den röda folkomröstningen<br/><br/>Naturligtvis skulle allt ovanstående förmodligen tjäna till att förklara varför en 2: a folkomröstning är en osannolik möjlighet, inte förrän efter brexit och skadan har skett. De två största partierna har mycket att förlora på en 2: a folkomröstning. Det skulle vara en dödsmatch för de politiska klasserna, en Game of Thrones säsongsfinal, med en kroppsräkning att matcha. Inte förrän vilda hästar drar dem till omröstningarna kommer vi att få en 2: a folkomröstning. Partipolitik kommer sannolikt att trumfa det nationella intresset. Och vad gäller folkets vilja, tror jag att budskapet från politiker nu sannolikt kommer att vara, för att parafrasera Boris, fu * k folket. När allt kommer omkring, om det motsatta var sant, skulle de aldrig ha hållit den första folkomröstningen!<br/><br/>Om man vill ha en andra röst måste man dra tillbaka sitt stöd från de stora partierna. Gå till din MP berätta för dem att du planerar att aldrig rösta på hans / hennes parti igen, även om du är i en marginell plats, kommer du att stödja ett pro 2: a röstparti från och med nu (lib dems, SNP, Greens). Först när tillräckligt många parlamentsledamöter börjar frukta för sina jobb finns det någon chans till en ny omröstning. |
|  148<br/>8<br/>Making ESA Decisions in the Face of Uncertainty<br/>The previous chapter described our ability to estimate the risk of extinction for populations of organisms.<br/>This is a major part of estimating the degree of endangerment of a population or species. In this chapter, we build<br/>on that information to discuss how important understanding and assessing risk is in ESA decision making and<br/>the question of whether different levels of risk should apply to different decisions. Finally, because decisions<br/>regarding endangered species must always be made in the face of uncertainty regarding estimates of extinction<br/>risk and future events, we suggest ways of improving agency decisions involving risk and uncertainty.<br/>DECISIONS REQUIRED UNDER THE ESA<br/>The objectives of the ESA are to conserve the ecosystems upon which endangered and threatened species<br/>depend, provide a program for the conservation of endangered and threatened species, and achieve the purposes<br/>of several international conservation agreements. While these objectives are not intrinsically or philosophically<br/>conflicting, they can conflict when agencies faced with limited budgets must decide how to apportion funds.<br/>More serious conflicts arise when the objectives of the act conflict with other human objectives, such as private-<br/>property rights and private and public developments. The act specifies the extent to which such conflicting<br/>objectives should be considered when making the different decisions required under the act. Consideration of<br/>human objectives other than those specified in the act is specifically prohibited when making decisions regarding<br/>list 149<br/>ing, take, and jeopardy, but is required when making decisions regarding critical habitat. Initial recovery<br/>planning is to be based solely on scientific considerations, but economic effects of the plan are to be considered<br/>before implementation.<br/>The terminology of the act implies that many decisions regarding conservation of species should consider<br/>estimates of extinction risk. Specific examples of such terminology include the definitions of endangered and<br/>threatened species, the provisions for removing species from the list, and the definitions of jeopardy on public<br/>lands and taking on private lands (see Box 8-1).<br/>THE NEED FOR NEW APPROACHES TO DECISION MAKING<br/>Agency decisions that have been taken under the direction of the act have been criticized by the general<br/>public (Mann and Plummer, 1992) and<br/>BOX 8-1 EXAMPLES OF TERMINOLOGY IN THE ESA (ITALICIZED) THAT IMPLIES AN<br/>ASSESSMENT OF THE DEGREE OF RISK TO A SPECIES<br/>ENDANGERED SPECIES: any species which is in danger of extinction throughout all or a significant<br/>portion of its range.<br/>THREATENED SPECIES: any species which is likely to become an endangered species within the<br/>foreseeable future throughout all or a significant portion of its range.<br/>CRITICAL HABITAT: the specific areas within the geographical area occupied by the species . . . on<br/>which are found those physical or biological features essential to the conservation of the species and which<br/>may require special management considerations or protection.<br/>REMOVAL FROM LIST (RECOVERY): [each recovery plan shall include] objective, measurable<br/>criteria which, when met, would result in a determination, in accordance with the provisions of this section,<br/>that the species be removed from the list.<br/>JEOPARDY ON PUBLIC LANDS: [each federal agency shall ensure that any action authorized,<br/>funded or carried out by such agency] is not likely to jeopardize the continued existence of any endangered<br/>species or threatened species or result in the destruction or adverse modification of habitat of such species<br/>which is determined to be critical.<br/>TAKE ON PRIVATE LANDS: . . . taking will not appreciably reduce the likelihood of the survival and<br/>recovery of the species in the wild 150<br/>the scientific community (Brownell et al., 1989; Goodman, 1993; Wilcove et al., 1993, Tear et al., 1993) for<br/>being arbitrary. For example, Brownell et al. (1989) pointed out that, at least for cetaceans, the list of threatened<br/>and endangered species is not scientifically defensible. Several large whales in little danger of extinction were on<br/>the list in 1989, although many small dolphins and porpoises with very small population sizes were not.<br/>Population size alone is not necessarily a sensitive indicator of extinction risk, but many species had declined to<br/>alarmingly low numbers by the time they were listed. Wilcove et al. (1993) found that the median population<br/>sizes of species when listed were about 1,000 individuals for animals and 120 individuals for plants.<br/>The backlog of candidate species awaiting listing and the number of legal actions taken related to listing<br/>decisions indicate that the current process for making listing decisions needs review and revision. The Fish and<br/>Wildlife Service (FWS) uses three categories to help make such decisions (see Box 8-2). Category 1 now<br/>contains about 400 species for which the agencies have substantial information to support the proposal to list but<br/>do not have sufficient resources to complete the process. Category 2 now contains about 3,500 species for which<br/>a petition to list might be justified but is deemed to be lacking in critical information. The large number of<br/>species in Category 2 indicates that the agencies have not developed a workable process for making listing<br/>decisions when faced with limited data, uncertainty, and limited financial and human resources. Indeed, lack of<br/>scientific information and uncertainty plague many public and private policy decisions.<br/>Recovery plans often fail to provide appropriate guidance on biologically reasonable levels of risk. A recent<br/>survey of recovery plans concluded that goals for species recovery were often unrealistically low and that these<br/>plans frequently manage for extinction rather than survival (Tear et al., 1993). For example, of the 54 threatened<br/>and endangered species for which population size data were available, 15 (28%) had recovery goals set at or<br/>below the existing population size at the time the plan was written (Tear et al., 1993). If the population was<br/>endangered at the time of listing—and the review of Wilcove et al. (1993) suggests that most listed populations<br/>are at risk—then such recovery goals are probably too low.<br/>The committee also notes that some recovered species have not been delisted or upgraded (i.e., from<br/>endangered to threatened) in a timely fashion. For example, eastern Pacific gray whales (Eschrictius robustus )<br/>were not delisted until 1994 (Fed. Reg. 59:31094), although population estimates were approaching the likely<br/>pre-exploitation sizes of 12,000-15,000 by the late 1970s and have continued to increase (Reilly, 1992). The<br/>comments about decisions to list also apply to decisions about delisting 151<br/>BOX 8-2 U.S. FISH AND WILDLIFE SERVICE DEFINITIONS FOR CATEGORIES 1, 2, AND 3,<br/>UNDER THE ENDANGERED SPECIES ACT (FED. REG. 58:51145, SEPT. 30, 1993).<br/>Category 1.—Taxa for which the Service has on file sufficient information on biological vulnerability<br/>and threat(s) to support proposals to list them as endangered or threatened species. Proposed rules have<br/>not yet been issued because this action is precluded at present by other listing activity. Development and<br/>publication of proposed rules on Category 1 taxa are anticipated, however, and the Service encourages<br/>other Federal agencies to give consideration to such taxa in environmental planning.<br/>Category 2.—Taxa for which information now in the possession of the Service indicates that proposing<br/>to list as endangered or threatened is possibly appropriate, but for which sufficient data on biological<br/>vulnerability and threat are not currently available to support proposed rules. The Service emphasizes that<br/>these taxa are not being proposed for listing by this notice, and that there are not current plans for such<br/>proposals unless additional supporting information becomes available. Further biological research and field<br/>study usually will be necessary to ascertain the status of taxa in this category. It is likely that many will be<br/>found not to warrant listing, either because they are not threatened or endangered or because they do not<br/>qualify as species under the definitions in the Act, while others will be found to be in greater danger of<br/>extinction than some taxa in Category 1.<br/>Category 3.—Taxa in Category 3 are not current candidates for listing. Such taxa are further divided<br/>into three subcategories to indicate the reason(s) for their removal from consideration:<br/>Category 3A.—Taxa for which the Service has persuasive evidence of extinction. If rediscovered,<br/>such taxa might acquire high priority for listing. At this time, however, the best available information<br/>indicates that the taxa in this subcategory, or the habitats from which they were known, have been lost.<br/>Category 3B.—Names that, on the basis of current taxonomic understanding (usually as represented<br/>in published revisions and monographs), do not represent distinct taxa meeting the Act's definition of<br/>"species." Such supposed taxa could be reevaluated in the future on the basis of new information.<br/>Category 3C.—Taxa that have proven to be more abundant or widespread than previously believed<br/>and/or those that are not subject to any identifiable threat. If further research or changes in habitat indicate<br/>a significant decline in any of these taxa, they may be reevaluated for possible inclusion in categories 1 or 2 152<br/>PROVIDING OBJECTIVE RISK STANDARDS<br/>The qualitative definitions in the act provide a framework for decision-making, that is, they provide a list of<br/>administrative and management actions and a general rationale for selecting each action. However, qualitative<br/>definitions alone can be interpreted in different ways by different people, and agencies have provided no<br/>guidance on the appropriate degrees of extinction risk for making the different decisions required by the act, such<br/>as listing a species as either threatened or endangered or declaring a species recovered. To ensure that ESA<br/>decisions protect endangered species as they are intended to and do so in a scientifically defensible way requires<br/>objective methods for assessing risk of extinction (see Chapter 7) and for assigning species to categories of<br/>protection according to their risk of extinction. Standards for assigning species to categories should be<br/>quantitative wherever possible, and, when this is not possible, qualitative procedures should at least be<br/>systematic and clearly defined.<br/>Developing Quantitative Risk Standards<br/>Agencies could achieve greater consistency in decisions if they provided quantitative risk standards for such<br/>terms as "endangered" and "threatened" to the many agency personnel involved in implementing the act. Risk<br/>levels should be defined as the probability of extinction within a specific time. As an example of quantitative<br/>guidance, Shaffer (1981) defined a minimum viable population size as that which would have at least a 99%<br/>chance of surviving for 1,000 years. The committee is not endorsing Shaffer's definition but noting that by<br/>providing quantitative guidance, Shaffer has made it possible to discuss or disagree with his definition<br/>objectively and apply the definition in a standard manner. As another example of a quantitative definition, Mace<br/>and Lande (1991) suggested that endangered could be defined as a 20% chance of extinction in 20 years.<br/>Time Frame for Estimating Risk of Extinction<br/>When providing a quantitative standard for assigning risk categories, risk of extinction must be defined with<br/>a specific time frame in mind, i.e., x probability of extinction in y years. Critical levels of extinction risk, which<br/>will trigger ESA decisions, such as listing or delisting, must be associated with a particular number of years or<br/>generations. As the time frame increases, the probability of extinction also increases, approaching 100% for all<br/>species if the period is long enough (see Chapter 2). The choice of a time frame for evaluating risk of extinction<br/>for purposes of the ESA reflects scientific and societal concerns. From a scientific standpoint, time should 153<br/>be long enough to guard against making management choices that might have favorable effects over the short<br/>term (e.g., the next 5 to 10 years), but consequent adverse effects over the longer term (e.g., the next 100 years or<br/>more). An example of such a choice might be management that extends the lifespan of adults currently in a<br/>population (enhancing short-term survival), but jeopardizes successful reproduction (leading to population<br/>decline as the current adult population ages and dies). Species' life-history characteristics help determine an<br/>appropriate time frame, with longer times being more appropriate for longer-lived species.<br/>Another scientific consideration is the time scale for natural cycles of disturbance and regeneration in the<br/>species' habitat. Evaluating the success of endangered species management over only a portion of the natural<br/>habitat cycle runs the risk of confusing natural fluctuations in population size with adverse reactions to<br/>management.<br/>Societal considerations regarding time frame include the desire to preserve species and their habitats over<br/>time scales meaningful to humans and their offspring, political cycles of 2-6 years, economic cycles, and many<br/>others. Because of the large variety of societal factors, this committee cannot specify all the appropriate time<br/>scales that should be considered in decision making or even the range of time scales for which extinction<br/>probabilities should be calculated. However, it is clear that the time scales implicit in the various calculations<br/>should be made explicit for informed decision making. In some cases, where there is an immediate and<br/>potentially reversible threat to species survival (such as a proposed development), it could make sense to analyze<br/>the probability of survival over a short period, perhaps 5 to 10 years or less, when comparing options for species<br/>recovery over the short term. Such an analysis should be followed, however, by another assessment of extinction<br/>risk over a longer period to ensure that short-term gains do not become long-term losses.<br/>Listing Systems Based on Objective Criteria and Rules<br/>A system for making listing decisions requires a set of objective criteria for assigning species to risk<br/>categories, such as endangered or threatened. The objective criteria most suitable for making listing decisions<br/>would be different degrees of extinction risk. However, we rarely have sufficient data to allow good estimates of<br/>extinction risk, so we need a system allowing the use of other criteria as well. Such a system could be based on<br/>objective criteria, such as some combination of population size and number, believed to represent a specific level<br/>of extinction risk.<br/>The need to develop a listing system based on objective criteria has been recognized by the principal<br/>international organization concerned with the listing and conservation of endangered species, the International<br/>Union 154<br/>for the Conservation of Nature (IUCN). In June 1992, the CITES (Convention on the International Trade in<br/>Endangered Species of Flora and Fauna) Standing Committee requested that IUCN help to develop new criteria<br/>for listing species in the appendices to the CITES treaty, which regulates trade in wildlife and wildlife products.<br/>The resulting proposed IUCN system for assessing degree of threat (Mace et al., 1992) was produced after an<br/>international effort by groups of scientists in a series of workshops. The proposed system is now being evaluated<br/>by members of the IUCN/SSC taxon specialist groups, various CITES committees, and interested scientists<br/>(Gnam, 1993) and was considered at the CITES 1994 meeting. At that meeting, a proposal was made to adopt<br/>the IUCN criteria; a counterproposal was made by the United States and a working group developed a<br/>compromise, which was recently accepted by CITES (Rosemarie Gnam, FWS, pers. commun., March 1, 1995).<br/>Much of the substance of the IUCN criteria remains in Annex 5 of the report as definitions, rather than the<br/>criteria for listing.<br/>In the proposed IUCN system, a species could be listed as endangered based on any of several criteria, each<br/>of which was intended to represent approximately the same risk of extinction. Decisions to list a species could be<br/>based on any of the following criteria: probability of extinction, trends in abundance, population size, number of<br/>populations, and geographical extent (Mace et al., 1992). The IUCN system was based on a combination of two<br/>previous approaches for assessing degree of threat: population-based criteria developed by those working with<br/>higher vertebrates and area-habitat criteria developed by those working with plants and invertebrates. Population-<br/>based criteria, known as the ''Mace-Lande criteria," were proposed in 1991 (Mace and Lande, 1991). They<br/>suggested three categories:<br/>Critical: 50% probability of extinction within 5 years or two generations, whichever is longer.<br/>Endangered: 20% probability of extinction within 20 years or 10 generations, whichever is longer.<br/>Vulnerable: 10% probability of extinction within 100 years.<br/>Unfortunately, due to the limited data on most taxa of conservation concern, a formal population viability<br/>analysis to estimate the probability of extinction in a particular case is often impossible. Moreover, population<br/>viability models for plants are poorly developed (Menges, 1990). To address this problem, Mace and Lande<br/>offered several surrogate criteria based on other types of information. For example, they considered that a<br/>population would qualify as endangered if the current population estimate was fewer than 250 mature<br/>individuals, even if insufficient demographic data were available to calculate an estimated probability of<br/>extinction. The current IUCN criteria rely heavily on population viability analysis, popula 155<br/>tion size, and range area. Concern has been expressed that these criteria omit historical data on a species'<br/>abundance and distribution, omit data on reproductive fitness, and ignore life cycles (Gnam, 1993). Furthermore,<br/>the IUCN system is still incomplete, because it lacks a set of rules to allow decisions to be made when there is<br/>uncertainty regarding the criteria used to categorize species according to relative risk of extinction. Nevertheless,<br/>those criteria represent the most important scientific effort to date to reach consensus on standard criteria for<br/>assigning taxa to threat categories in a uniform, objective manner.<br/>The adoption of a similar system in the U.S. would make listing decisions more consistent. Unfortunately,<br/>there is as yet no evidence that the criteria used in the IUCN system do represent comparable degrees of risk.<br/>Any system of criteria developed for use with the ESA should be thoroughly tested against a variety of<br/>population and metapopulation models before implementation. Designing and testing an appropriate system for<br/>listing species is a formidable scientific task best accomplished by an independent scientific committee. For<br/>example, for a much simpler problem, management of the commercial harvest of whales, the Scientific<br/>Committee, of the International Whaling Commission took 6 years to evaluate five alternative proposed<br/>management procedures (Donovan, 1989; R. Brownell, pers. commun., NMFS, 1994).<br/>Limitations on Estimates of Risk<br/>Our ability to estimate risk of extinction for use in assigning species to protection categories is limited by<br/>our understanding of the factors influencing extinction. Two areas where we are acutely aware of limitations are<br/>in models used to estimate risk of extinction and in our understanding of the role of critical thresholds of risk and<br/>of cumulative effects of risk factors.<br/>Limits of Models<br/>As described in Chapter 7, models for estimating risk of extinction are limited in their ability to incorporate<br/>the full complexity of species population dynamics. Estimates of risk derived from these models may reflect only<br/>a subset of the factors actually influencing a species' risk of extinction. As discussed in Chapter 7, it seems likely<br/>that the simplifications and omissions of current models can underestimate risk of extinction.<br/>Poor Understanding of Cumulative Effects and Thresholds<br/>Decisions under the ESA regarding take or jeopardy require making decisions regarding incremental risks<br/>of extinction. Assessing the added 156<br/>risk from specific human actions is usually an even more difficult task than estimating the overall extinction risk<br/>to a species. Individual human actions, such as developing a few acres of habitat, pose small incremental risks of<br/>extinction. At some point, however, the small incremental risks from numerous human actions, if not stemmed,<br/>will accumulate so as to produce a major effect. Not enough is known about cumulative effects and threshold<br/>points to make accurate risk predictions possible (e.g., Beanlands et al., 1986), although there has been some<br/>theoretical work on critical thresholds of habitat patch size or fragmentation (e.g., Lande, 1988 for spotted owl<br/>habitat fragmentation). When considering the probable effects of incremental human activities, it is reasonable to<br/>assume that additional activity means additional risk, but we rarely know whether the relationship between<br/>additional activity and additional risk is linear or whether there might be critical levels of activity above which<br/>the risk of extinction increases dramatically.<br/>Should Different Risk Standards Apply to Different ESA Decisions?<br/>Endangered, Threatened, Recovered<br/>In judging whether different levels of risk should apply to different types of decisions under the act, the<br/>committee considered carefully the terminology of the act shown in Box 8-1. The definition of an endangered<br/>species as one that is already in danger of extinction and a threatened species as one that is likely to become an<br/>endangered species implies that a species listed as endangered is at greater risk of extinction than a threatened<br/>one. The determination that a species should be removed from the list implies that its risk of extinction has<br/>decreased to the point where it is no longer considered threatened. Thus, it is clear that determinations of a single<br/>species as successively endangered, threatened, and recovered should represent a series of decreasing levels of<br/>risk of extinction faced by the species.<br/>Different Taxa<br/>Although cross-species comparisons are complicated by many factors specific to the biology of individual<br/>species, it is appropriate to set the same degree of risk as a standard for listing any species, whether plant or<br/>animal, as endangered and another, somewhat lower degree of risk, for listing any species as threatened. Thus it<br/>is reasonable to expect that species determined to be endangered should, on the average, face a greater risk of<br/>extinction than those determined to be threatened. This seemingly obvious 157<br/>ordering of risk has not always been followed in practice (Wilcove et al., 1993).<br/>Public Versus Private Land<br/>Controversy has arisen over whether the inclusion of habitat destruction or modification as a form of taking<br/>under Section 9 sets a different standard of responsibility for protection of endangered species by private versus<br/>public entities. (We note again that this interpretation of taking is under court review at this writing.) In<br/>particular, it has appeared to some that the standard of responsibility might be interpreted to be more stringent<br/>for private than for public entities. This seems, if anything, the reverse of what was intended by the ESA. From a<br/>scientific perspective, actions resulting in a given degree of risk of extinction are equally hazardous to species<br/>whether they are carried out by public or private entities on public or on private land. The committee sees no<br/>scientific reason for setting different standards for categorizing risk of extinction under different sections of the<br/>act. However, because public and private entities behave differently, achieving the same degree of biological<br/>protection on public versus private lands does not necessarily imply identical regulatory requirements on behalf<br/>of species experiencing the same risk of extinction on public and on private lands.<br/>USING STRUCTURED APPROACHES TO DECISION MAKING<br/>Why use structured approaches to ESA decision making? Because the issues are complex and relevant<br/>scientific data are often fragmentary, it can be difficult to make decisions regarding endangered or threatened<br/>species. Decisions regarding endangered species are often characterized by insufficient data, probabilistic<br/>predictions regarding future events, considerable uncertainty regarding the accuracy of these predictions,<br/>conflicting management objectives, disagreement over the best course of action, and the need to justify whatever<br/>decision is made.<br/>Given these problems, it is important that we try to make our decision-making process as explicit as<br/>possible, especially because research on the psychology of human decision making (Hogarth, 1980; Kahneman<br/>et al., 1982; von Winterfeldt and Edwards, 1986) has shown that intuitive decisions exhibit many inconsistencies<br/>and biases, particularly when probabilistic information and multiple objectives are involved. The use of an<br/>explicit framework to guide decisions can help us make choices that are consistent with our goals, data, and<br/>beliefs and facilitate compromise among those with differing views. Several techniques developed in the fields<br/>of operations research and management science—the academic disciplines dealing with scientific approaches to<br/>decision making—provide helpful frameworks 158<br/>for making these difficult decisions. These techniques help decision makers think about the decision in a<br/>systematic way; break down difficult decisions into a series of smaller, easier decisions; and document the<br/>process used to reach the decision, which makes the decision easier to justify and defend.<br/>Some applications of these structured approaches, particularly the qualitative ones, might appear to be<br/>simple pleas for clear thinking, and they are. But clear thinking does not come easily or naturally in the face of<br/>scientific complexity and uncertainty, competing objectives within recovery programs and beyond, and political<br/>pressures from multiple constituencies. We propose decision analysis as an example of a structured problem-<br/>solving method, although other methods (such as the Analytic Hierarchy Process (Saaty, 1990)) or approaches<br/>based on goal programming and multiattribute ranking (Ralls and Starfield, 1995) can be useful as well. We<br/>stress the merits of using these tools as conceptual frameworks, not just as number-crunching devices. And we<br/>emphasize that using these approaches is not necessarily a call for more information, but rather for more<br/>coherent use of existing information.<br/>Using Subjective Probability and Expert Opinion<br/>In some cases, there is very little "hard" information that seems relevant to estimating the risks affecting<br/>endangered species, but some experts have accumulated experience that allows them to make informed<br/>judgments about these risks. Such expert judgment is so often available for endangered species decisions that it<br/>is of great benefit to have orderly methods of eliciting and using it for decision making. One of the strengths of<br/>decision analysis is its ability to estimate "subjective probabilities" and then use them for analysis in the same<br/>way that long-term frequency estimates would be used (Behn and Vaupel, 1982; von Winterfeldt and Edwards,<br/>1986; Maguire, 1987). The methods allow a concrete expression of expert opinion, facilitating scrutiny by the<br/>public and comparison with other views.<br/>Another place where expert opinion can be essential is in cases when some background information is<br/>available, but unique aspects of a situation differentiate it from similar situations that have occurred. An example<br/>might be assessing the likelihood of successful reproduction in a captive population where experience has been<br/>dismal but new reproductive technologies have been developed that might prove helpful, as for example with the<br/>endangered Hawaiian crow (NRC, 1992a). In this case, it is relevant to amend or update the historical<br/>information in light of the new techniques. There are formal ways to combine old data with new opinion (and<br/>new data with old opinion) using Bayesian estimation (Raiffa, 1968; Clemen, 1991 159<br/>Linking Science and Values<br/>Even though estimates of risk are grounded in scientific information, those implementing the act often make<br/>value judgments when making decisions about listing, jeopardy, etc. They are deciding which species to list<br/>quickly and which to relegate to delayed listing, which areas of habitat are worth the socioeconomic cost or<br/>political effort to designate as critical and which are not, what degree of jeopardy is worth altering a federally<br/>funded development project for and what is not. Many citizens are willing to allow public officials to make such<br/>judgments on their behalf, but those involved might be more comfortable if the values informing those<br/>judgments and their effects on ESA decisions were articulated more clearly.<br/>A hallmark of formal decision analysis (Behn and Vaupel, 1982; Clemen, 1991) and other structured<br/>problem-solving methods (Ralls and Starfield, 1995) is an emphasis on articulating clearly the objectives for a<br/>decision and criteria for evaluating how well alternative proposals might meet those objectives. Use of such<br/>methods can improve ESA decisions by making the connection between values, objectives, and decisions more<br/>transparent, helping to disarm criticisms that the government is capricious or partisan in implementing the act<br/>(Mann and Plummer, 1992; Tear et al., 1993).<br/>Making good use of science, as instructed in the ESA, requires making appropriate connections between the<br/>values and objectives being pursued in a decision and the scientific evidence and reasoning used to evaluate<br/>alternative ways of meeting those objectives. Science by itself is not sufficient input to policy decisions, apart<br/>from the objectives and values it serves. Articulating an explicit framework can help link science and values and<br/>lead to better and more defensible decisions.<br/>Scientific Uncertainty in ESA Decisions<br/>For even the best-studied endangered species, essential pieces of information might be lacking, yet<br/>decisions must be made. Sometimes, it is possible to delay action while gathering better information, although<br/>that strategy carries its own risks. Sometimes, important factors affecting how management actions turn out, such<br/>as catastrophic weather conditions or pollution accidents, are inherently uncertain, and no amount of further<br/>study could do more than improve the accuracy and precision of estimates of their likelihoods. In any case,<br/>weighing the best choice under uncertainty about outcomes is a necessity. This kind of probabilistic reasoning<br/>does not come naturally and many managers are uncomfortable with it, resorting to shortcut heuristics to<br/>simplify information and justify their choices (von Winterfeldt and Edwards, 1986). The framework of decision<br/>analysis offers a structure 160<br/>for considering probabilistic information in a coherent and consistent way, providing better use of whatever<br/>information is available to guide decisions.<br/>Estimating Uncertain Quantities<br/>Several types of information bearing on ESA decisions can be uncertain, including factors influencing how<br/>management actions turn out (such as weather events or sociopolitical events) and measures of outcome (such as<br/>the likelihood of population persistence under a particular management strategy). Such probabilistic quantities<br/>enter the analysis in different ways, but in both cases, methods of estimating those quantities are needed.<br/>Sometimes, relevant long-term data on the frequency of an uncertain event can be used to estimate its<br/>probability; as an example, there are weather records for severe storms in particular coastal areas. These could be<br/>used to estimate the likelihood of a tropical storm striking the Gulf Coast of Texas near the wintering ground for<br/>the migratory whooping crane population or the probability that a hurricane would destroy the habitat of some<br/>red-cockaded woodpeckers. In one case, population models were used to help decisions concerning conservation<br/>of endangered sea turtles by identifying the most sensitive life stages (NRC, 1992b).<br/>In other cases, no useful long-term data are available, but there might be models incorporating our best<br/>understanding of the factors affecting the likelihood of an uncertain event. Examples are stochastic population<br/>models, such as are discussed in Chapter 7. Those use information about demographic parameters and<br/>environmental factors to predict probability of extinction for a population with particular characteristics.<br/>Reducing Uncertainty by Gathering Information<br/>Listing actions, recovery plans, or other ESA decisions often are delayed due to inadequate information.<br/>Those implementing the act almost always believe that with additional information, they could make a better<br/>decision. Nevertheless, decisions to delay action pending further information and directives to gather additional<br/>information should be viewed critically. What kind of information and of what quality could be gathered within<br/>the time and resources available? What are the possible answers that such investigation might reveal? What<br/>decisions would be triggered by different answers? How are those decisions different from those that would be<br/>made using existing information? What effect will continuing the status quo have on species status and on<br/>options for future action? Considering these questions in a structured framework can make it more likely that a<br/>reasonable decision will be made.<br/>An example of such an analysis comes from Bart and Robson (in press 161<br/>They analyzed the variability in raptor population data to find out how many years of data would be<br/>required to tell whether the northern spotted owl population was stable or increasing, a criterion for delisting<br/>specified in the spotted owl recovery plan. Their analysis showed that it would not be possible to render a sound<br/>judgment about delisting based on fewer than 8 years of population monitoring data. (See Box 8-3 on statistical<br/>power and types of errors.) Such an analysis promotes realistic expectations about the time and effort required to<br/>obtain a satisfactory answer and forestalls charges that the FWS is delaying delisting for nonscientific reasons.<br/>Sometimes a qualitative but orderly consideration of questions is sufficient to guide action, giving managers<br/>the confidence either to pursue additional information or proceed on the basis of the information they already<br/>have. At other times, a more formal analysis of the value of information (Raiffa, 1968; Clemen, 1991) might be<br/>needed. In either case, the scientific uncertainty must be examined within the context set by the objectives for a<br/>particular situation. The question of how many years of monitoring data would be required to make a decision<br/>about delisting the northern spotted owl can be answered only with reference to the level of confidence required,<br/>which can be determined only with reference to the objectives of spotted owl management (Taylor and<br/>Gerrodette, 1993).<br/>More information almost always seems better to those trained as cautious natural resource scientists. Yet,<br/>too much risk aversion, or fear of making the wrong decision based on limited information, can be crippling. The<br/>California condor and the black-footed ferret are good examples. In these cases, and in many similar ones<br/>worldwide, a management decision to remove individuals from the wild to begin or to augment a captive-<br/>breeding effort would have an indisputably negative effect on the survival of the population in the wild<br/>(Maguire, 1986; 1989). Such a decision must be justified in terms of the likelihood of extinction of the<br/>population even if no removals were made, and the long-term benefit to species survival from a captive breeding<br/>program, if successful. In the condor and ferret programs, only when it became clear that a continuation of status-<br/>quo management of the wild population would lead to disaster did the negative consequences of removals<br/>become acceptable. But by that point, it was very nearly too late to make a success of the captive-breeding<br/>programs (Ralls and Ballou, 1992). Although both programs were ultimately successful in producing animals for<br/>reintroduction, neither case can be cited as a model of informed decision making under uncertainty. In both<br/>cases, too much attention was given to the possible negative consequences of the novel strategy (capturing<br/>animals from the wild) and too little to the possible negative consequences of continuing the conservative<br/>approach (trying to protect the dwindling wild populations 162<br/>BOX 8-3 STATISTICAL POWER AND TYPES OF ERRORS<br/>Statistical tests are used to control the likelihood of drawing erroneous conclusions about scientific<br/>hypotheses from data. The null hypothesis usually states that there is no difference between two or more)<br/>populations with regard to some characteristic in which we are interested. In contrast, the alternative<br/>hypothesis states that there is a difference in that particular characteristic.<br/>The alternative hypothesis is the more important one from the practical point of view because we<br/>would be likely to take some management action if it were true. However, the rules of statistics require that<br/>we test the null hypothesis rather than the alternative hypothesis. in other words, we make an "argument by<br/>contradiction:" we assume that the null hypothesis is true and then see if the data enable us to conclude<br/>that it is actually highly likely to be false. If so, we conclude that the alternative hypothesis is likely to be<br/>true. On the other hand, if we cannot show that the null hypothesis is likely to be false, we conclude that the<br/>alternative hypothesis is unlikely to be true.<br/>Because statistical tests merely allow us to estimate the probability that a hypothesis is true given a<br/>particular set of observations, two types of mistakes are possible. The first possible mistake is that we<br/>might conclude that the null hypothesis is false when in fact it is true. This is called rejecting a true null<br/>hypothesis or making a Type 1 error. The second possible mistake is that we might conclude that the null<br/>hypothesis is true when in fact it is false. This is called accepting a false null hypothesis or making a Type 2<br/>error.<br/>A specific example may be helpful. Suppose we would like to know whether or not the population of an<br/>endangered species is smaller in 1990 than it was in 1980. There would be no need for a statistical<br/>analysis if we could count every individual: if the number counted in 1990 was smaller than the number<br/>counted in 1980 we could conclude with certainty that the population had decreased over this time period.<br/>The need for statistics arises because we cannot count every individual; we can only conduct surveys and<br/>make estimates of population size. These estimates might be very precise, that is we might have a low<br/>degree of uncertainty about them. Usually, however, we are only able to make somewhat imprecise<br/>estimates about which we have a considerable degree of uncertainty. Regardless of the degree of precision<br/>of our estimates, we must consider a probability distribution of possible population sizes in 1990 and<br/>another probability distribution of population sizes in 1980 (Figure 8-1a for a more precise estimate and<br/>Figure 8-1b for a less precise estimate).<br/>The null hypothesis is that there is no difference between the population size in 1980 and that in 1990.<br/>The alternative hypothesis is that the population size in 1990 is smaller than the population size in 1980.<br/>We test the null hypothesis that there is no difference between the population sizes in 1980 and in 1990.<br/>Three things determine our ability to say whether the 1990 population is smaller than the 1980<br/>population: the magnitude of the difference between our two population estimates; the precision of our<br/>population estimates; and the risk we are willing to take that we falsely conclude the 1990 population is<br/>smaller. The situation with very precise population estimates is shown in 163<br/>Figure 8-1a. Looking at the distribution of population estimates for 1980, we see that 1,000 is the most<br/>likely population size but that there is some probability of many other population sizes, some of which<br/>overlap with the range of possible population sizes for 1990. The scientist must decide what estimate for<br/>1990 is sufficiently unlikely to be part of the 1980 distribution to warrant the conclusion that the two are<br/>really different.<br/>Scientists are usually cautious about such decisions. If the efficacy of a drug were being tested, it<br/>would not contribute to scientific progress if scientists often said a drug did have an effect when it did not.<br/>For this reason, the standard risk taken of falsely rejecting the null hypothesis is 1 in 20 (usually phrased<br/>). In our population example, that means we would reject the null hypothesis for any 1990<br/>population estimate less than the value shown by the dark vertical bar in Figure 8-1a. The area of the 1980<br/>distribution smaller than that value is 5% of the distribution (1/20th).<br/>Now consider our 1990 estimate. The most likely population size is 800. We want to know if the 1990<br/>population is smaller: has our endangered species continued to decline? Presumably, if the population has<br/>decreased, a different management action will be taken than if the population had remained stable. Unlike<br/>our drug test, there is a good argument that the cost of incorrectly concluding that the population has not<br/>declined (Type 2 error) is greater than the cost of incorrectly concluding that there has been a decline<br/>(Type 1 error).<br/>The probability of correctly rejecting the null hypothesis is the power of the test. In Figure 8-1a, any<br/>1990 population estimate less than the critical value would lead to rejection of the null hypothesis.<br/>Therefore, the area of the 1990 distribution to the left of the vertical bar is the power of the test. Figure 8-1b<br/>shows estimates with somewhat lower precision. Power has decreased to a worrisome point: we have only<br/>an 11% chance of making a correct decision that the population has declined. If the scientist is willing to<br/>take a higher risk of falsely rejecting the null hypothesis, power will be increased. The thin line in<br/>Figure 8-1b shows a 1/4 chance ( ) of a Type 1 error, and power is raised from 11% to 50%. If the<br/>scientist is willing to make Type 1 errors 50% of the time ( , reject the null hypothesis for any 1990<br/>estimate less than 1,000), then power is raised to 80%.<br/>Figure 8-2 shows the tradeoff between power and Type 1 errors for estimates of different precision.<br/>Population estimates and other data on endangered species are often poor, which means that the failure to<br/>reject the null hypothesis (a Type 2 error) often stems from inadequate statistical power rather than any<br/>basis in fact. For example, a recent review of the status of 20 cetacean stocks off the coast of California<br/>(Barlow et al., 1993) found that only two stocks had population estimates with coefficients of variation of<br/>less than 0.3—the median level of precision shown in Figure 8-2—while 11 had population estimates with<br/>coefficients of variation between 0.3 and 0. 8 and 7 had population estimates with coefficients of variation<br/>even greater than 0.8 (the low level of precision in Figure 8-2). However, those testing hypotheses<br/>regarding endangered species often fail to calculate whether they have adequate statistical power (Taylor<br/>and Gerodette, 1993Figure 8-1<br/>MAKING ESA DECISIONS IN THE FACE OF UNCERTAINTY<br/>Power of statistical tests to detect changes in population<br/>size. (Top) Comparison of populations with precise size estimates.<br/>(Bottom) Comparison of populations with less precise size estimates.<br/>164 165<br/>Figure 8-2<br/>Tradeoffs between statistical power and probability of making Type 1 errors for population-<br/>size estimates of varying precision.<br/>Types of Errors<br/>In the many cases where it is not possible to gather and analyze more information before making a decision,<br/>using a formal structure like decision analysis can help managers consider explicitly the ways in which they<br/>might be wrong in their predictions (e.g., about whether a particular population is genetically distinct from others<br/>or whether loss of a particular habitat area will lead to extinction) and the biological and socioeconomic<br/>consequences of being wrong in various ways (such as failing to predict extinction when it will in fact occur, or<br/>declaring a population genetically distinct when it is not). Many times the consequences of being wrong are<br/>highly asymmetrical; that is, one type of error is much more serious for the species than the converse, and<br/>perhaps even irreversible (Box 8-3, Table 8-1). Taylor and Gerrodette (1993) reinforced this point in their<br/>discussion of using statistical power analyses to design and evaluate monitoring schemes for endangered species.<br/>use the print version of this publication as the authoritative version for attribution.<br/>Analyzing such situations using the framework of decision analysis helps ensure that all types of errors are<br/>considered and that actions chosen are responsive to the likelihood of being wrong and its associated conse 166<br/>quences. The ferret and condor capture programs illustrate this point. Managers concerned with delisting a<br/>species must be wary of two types of errors: delisting when the population is actually declining (Type I error)<br/>and failing to delist when the population is actually stable or increasing (Type II error). (Box 8-3 discusses each<br/>type of error in relation to statistical power.) Each type of error has biological and nonbiological consequences,<br/>and they are asymmetrical (Table 8-1). The former error has adverse biological consequences for the species—it<br/>would be irreversible if the species became extinct—and, perhaps, positive socioeconomic consequences for<br/>sectors whose actions are constrained by recovery guidelines. The latter has neutral to positive consequences for<br/>the species and possible continued negative socioeconomic consequences. To set acceptable error rates for each<br/>type of error, which will in turn determine how many years of monitoring data are required to inform a delisting<br/>decision, the magnitude and likelihood of positive and negative consequences using biological and<br/>socioeconomic measures must be weighed in a decision analysis framework. These decisions are complicated<br/>and consequential enough that unaided intuition cannot always be trusted to do a good job.<br/>TABLE 8-1 Consequences of Making Two Types of Statistical Errors When Evaluating Scientific Data on Endangered<br/>Species<br/>Type 1 Error Type 2 Error<br/>Reject true null hypothesis Accept false null hypothesis<br/>Claim an effect when none exists Claim no effect when one exists<br/>Protect species more than necessary Protect species less than necessary, even lose species<br/>Lose scientific credibility Lose practical, and scientific credibility<br/>Increase socioeconomic costs more than necessary Permit activities that should not have been approved<br/>Source: Adapted from Noss (1992).<br/>To understand the types of errors that can be made when evaluating scientific data on endangered species<br/>and the consequences of these errors, it is helpful to understand the basic logic used when testing hypotheses<br/>(Box 8-3). Because statistical tests do not tell us that a hypothesis is true in an absolute sense but merely allow us<br/>to control the probability of error in our judgments about whether a hypothesis is true, two types of mistakes are<br/>always possible: we might conclude that a hypothesis is false when it is true, or we might conclude that a<br/>hypothesis is true when it is false (Box 8-3).<br/>use the print version of this publication as the authoritative version for attribution.<br/><br/>About this PDF file: This new digital representation of the original work has been recomposed from XML files created from the original paper book 167<br/>Scientists must try to avoid both types of errors. However, it is impossible to simultaneously minimize the<br/>likelihood of making both types of errors. The more we try to avoid making the first type of error, the more<br/>likely it is that we will make the second type. Scientists are trained to minimize the probability of making the<br/>first error, that is, rejecting a null hypothesis when it is actually true (Box 8-3). This choice is appropriate for<br/>advancing scientific knowledge, but it might not be the best for making management decisions. If not examined<br/>explicitly, this asymmetric error structure can bias decisions under the act to the detriment of endangered species,<br/>especially if they are based on analyses that do not take the asymmetric risk function into account. One situation<br/>where this can occur is listing decisions for species where information on population status is limited, a common<br/>occurrence. If a statistical analysis is performed, the trigger for listing is rejection of the null hypothesis that the<br/>species is not endangered. Typical error rates for such statistical tests of hypotheses keep the likelihood of false<br/>rejection low, but at the expense of substantial risk of falsely concluding that a species is not endangered. In the<br/>absence of conclusive evidence that a species is in fact endangered, uncertainty about status can result in<br/>acceptance of the null hypothesis, whether true or not. This results in an asymmetric risk function for the species<br/>(i.e., the probability that the species will not be protected when protection is needed is greater than the<br/>probability that the species will be protected unnecessarily), because the null hypothesis is usually that a<br/>population has not declined or that a specific action will have no effect (Noss, 1992; Taylor and Gerrodette,<br/>1993). Furthermore, limited data often result in inadequate statistical power. Thus, the null hypothesis of no<br/>impact on an endangered species might not be rejected when it should have been (Taylor and Gerrodette 1993).<br/>As a result, conservation measures that should be undertaken will not be.<br/>Burden of Proof<br/>In the section above on types of errors that might be made when making decisions under uncertainty, we<br/>have shown that the consequences of different types of errors might be asymmetrical, such that it is more<br/>important to avoid one type of error than another. We have suggested that ESA decisions should explicitly<br/>recognize that the consequences of different types of errors can differ and design decision-making procedures<br/>accordingly. An aspect of these decision-making procedures that we would like to emphasize here is the issue of<br/>''burden of proof." By this, we mean presumptions about what a party to an ESA decision should have to<br/>demonstrate to trigger protective actions. We are concerned that some current procedures may, perhaps<br/>inadvertently, bias decision-making in ways that are not intended under the act 168<br/>Statistical Errors<br/>In the usual procedures for formulating scientific tests of hypotheses, it is customary to phrase the null<br/>hypothesis as the "no effect" case (e.g., a proposed action will not affect the survival of a listed species) and to<br/>use confidence levels that limit the probability of falsely rejecting that null hypothesis to a known level (often,<br/>0.05) while permitting much larger probabilities of falsely accepting the null hypothesis. We are concerned that<br/>when such statistical procedures are followed in ESA decision-making, they will too often place the burden of<br/>proof (for demonstrating a significant effect) on those who want to institute some protective action (usually the<br/>FWS or petitioners for listing of a species), without taking into account the practical consequences of falsely<br/>concluding that no effect is occurring. This could lead to a systematic bias against species that are candidates for<br/>listing or for listed species in need of protective actions.<br/>Cumulative Effects/Thresholds<br/>One situation where this type of problem could arise is when those concerned with species protection<br/>suspect that there might be a critical threshold of effect (above which the risk of extinction might increase<br/>dramatically) or where cumulative effects might push a species past such a threshold. We have indicated above<br/>that our technical ability to predict such thresholds is very limited. If the burden of proof is on those who must<br/>show that such a threshold exists (and where it is and just what increase in risk of extinction will occur), there<br/>will be few instances in which such a threshold can be demonstrated. As a solution to this problem, we are not<br/>advocating that such thresholds simply be assumed unless proven otherwise (which would reverse the burden of<br/>proof), but rather that the consequences of each type of error (failing to identify a threshold when one actually<br/>exists versus assuming a threshold when one does not exist) be examined to design a decision-making procedure<br/>that properly controls the risk of errors, from the point of view of species protection and from the point of view<br/>of avoiding unneeded constraints on other interests. In other words, it is advantageous to make the assumptions<br/>and their predicted consequences explicit.<br/>Listing Decisions<br/>Another area where we are concerned about asymmetric risk functions for endangered species is in<br/>decisions to list them. Lack of information can work against species at risk at the listing stage. Under current<br/>conditions, FWS resources for evaluating information on candidate species and for gathering additional<br/>information to make a decision are severely limited 169<br/>The solution is not simply to reverse the burden of proof and confer protection on all species proposed for<br/>listing but to consider explicitly the consequences of both types of error: of listing species that are not really<br/>endangered and of failing to list those that really are. Listing decisions are not one of the points in ESA decision<br/>making where socioeconomic consequences are to be weighed against species protection, so these need not be<br/>part of the equation for determining where the burden of proof should lie for a particular case. However, we<br/>acknowledge that with limited time and money for reviewing the eligibility of species for listing, only those<br/>species whose situations are known to be the most desperate will receive priority.<br/>Reducing Asymmetry of Risk for Listed Species<br/>In addition to concerns about risks for species at the point of listing, we are also concerned that similar<br/>asymmetry of risk functions can occur during decisions regarding protection of already listed species. The usual<br/>way of deciding whether there is likely to be a harmful effect is to pose the null hypothesis of no harm and set a<br/>low (usually 0.05) rate of error for falsely concluding that there will be harm. This way of framing the question,<br/>in combination with limited information on the effects of habitat alteration on listed species, is more likely to<br/>deny needed protection than to afford unneeded protection. If the burden of proof were to show that an action<br/>would not harm a species rather than to show that it would harm a species, increased protection would result.<br/>The importance of shifting the burden of proof this way has been widely recognized, especially in the context of<br/>marine conservation issues, and is known as "the precautionary principle" (Cameron and Werksman, 1991;<br/>Porter and Brown, 1991; Earll, 1992; Norse, 1993). This principle has already been endorsed in several<br/>international legal documents (Porter and Brown, 1991). Recently, the National Marine Fisheries Service<br/>(NMFS) explicitly took this kind of asymmetry and the potential for irreversibility into account in deciding to list<br/>the anadromous Snake River sockeye salmon (Oncorhynchus nerka) as endangered in the face of uncertainty<br/>(Waples, in press). The uncertainty concerned whether the anadromous form, which spawns in Redfish Lake,<br/>Idaho, was genetically identical to the landlocked form of O. nerka, the kokanee, which is common in Redfish<br/>Lake. The decision to list1 was based in part on "the recognition that the consequences of taking the alternate<br/>course (i.e., assuming that recent anadromous [fish] in Redfish Lake were derived from<br/>use the print version of this publication as the authoritative version for attribution.<br/>1Fed. Reg. 56:58619, Nov. 20, 1991. Information obtained after the decision indicated that the anadromous form was<br/>indeed genetically different from the landlocked form (Waples, in press). 170<br/>kokanee) and being wrong were irreversible, since the original sockeye salmon gene pool could easily become<br/>extinct before the mistake was realized."<br/>Making Tradeoffs Among Competing Objectives<br/>There are always too few resources for the size of the job, and the government has been criticized for<br/>allocating funds for species protection without regard for its own stated priorities (Mann and Plummer, 1992).<br/>Most people recognize that not everything can be done at once, but these limitations probably would be more<br/>acceptable if there were a clearer connection between objectives being pursued and actions taken. Although the<br/>language of the ESA suggests that the standards for making decisions about listing, jeopardy, etc., are to be<br/>purely scientific, analyses of ESA implementation (e.g., Yaffee, 1982) show clearly that tradeoffs among<br/>conflicting objectives must be made in almost every instance. In a few cases, these conflicting objectives and the<br/>necessity for balancing them are made explicit in the act and its implementing regulations. For example, in<br/>treating critical habitat, the act (Section 4(b)(2)) recognizes that designating critical habitat might have<br/>socioeconomic costs and directs those implementing the act to weigh benefits to the listed species against these<br/>costs. Similarly, the exemption process specifically directs the exemption committee to ask whether there is an<br/>overriding benefit to society from the proposed project that would justify its approval, despite its threat to listed<br/>species.<br/>In most cases, however, tradeoffs among competing objectives arise in the course of implementing the act<br/>with too few resources, whether financial, human, or natural. For example, there are almost 4,000 candidates for<br/>listing (categories 1 and 2), not all of which can be acted on at once. Those responsible for listing decisions must<br/>decide which to consider first and which to delay, based on their best judgments about immediacy of threat,<br/>distinctiveness of the taxonomic group, etc. Again, structured decision-making techniques can be helpful when<br/>deciding on the best use of limited resources. Thibodeau's (1983) application of decision analysis to choose sites<br/>for a recovery program is an early application of a structured problem-solving technique to allocate scarce<br/>financial resources for conservation. Maguire and Lacy (1990) used decision analysis to help allocate limited zoo<br/>space among tiger subspecies in need of captive conservation. To make clear choices among competing<br/>objectives and to justify those choices to interested publics, it would be helpful to follow a more explicit<br/>framework for evaluating tradeoffs, such as that included in the repertoire of multiattribute decision analysis<br/>(Keeney and Raiffa, 1976; Keeney, 1992), which has been applied to several endangered-species problems<br/>(Maguire, 1986; Maguire and Servheen, 1992; Ralls and Starfield, 1995 171<br/>Among the criteria appropriate for setting priorities among species (or other units of protection), all of<br/>which qualify for listing or recovery, are those reflecting how protection of a particular taxon might contribute to<br/>maintenance of biological diversity (see Chapter 3). Distinctiveness of various sorts is one measure of this<br/>contribution:<br/>Genetic. Does the taxon contain genetic material not represented elsewhere that could provide raw material<br/>valuable for adaptation and evolution in future environments?<br/>Phylogenetic. Does the taxon represent a branch of the evolutionary tree of life that has few or no other<br/>living members?<br/>Ecological. Does the taxon exhibit an unusual adaptation to its environment, particularly to a rare habitat<br/>type (such as vernal pools or hot springs), or does it participate in an unusually close interdependence with other<br/>rare (i.e., threatened, endangered, or candidate) species (such as obligate mutualists or parasites), or does it have<br/>critical functional roles (i.e., is it a "keystone" species)?<br/>In addition to measures of distinctiveness, other considerations in setting priorities among units of<br/>protection include the degree to which conserving that taxon would enhance protection of overall diversity.<br/>Higher priority would thus be given to taxa with unusually high levels of genetic diversity, to ecosystems with<br/>high levels of endemism, and to taxa whose demise would be likely to precipitate further extinctions of taxa<br/>dependent on them. Finally, there are species often referred to as "umbrella species," i.e., they are species whose<br/>protection entails the protection of habitats and ecosystems that would confer protection on other (endangered)<br/>species. Clearly, if priorities have to be set, an umbrella species should receive a high priority.<br/>FWS (FWS, 1983) and NMFS (NOAA, 1990) have developed hierarchical systems for determining listing,<br/>delisting and reclassification, and species-recovery priorities; the NMFS systems are simplified versions of the<br/>FWS systems. The FWS system for listing priorities considers the magnitude of the threat to the species, the<br/>immediacy of the threat to the species, and the distinctiveness of the species based on its taxonomy, e.g., a<br/>monotypic genus is given a higher priority than a species, which in turn is given a higher priority than a<br/>subspecies. The system for setting recovery priorities also uses taxonomic level as an indicator of distinctiveness.<br/>However, as explained in Chapter 3, taxonomic ranking does not necessarily reflect the same degree of<br/>phylogenetic distinctiveness among all groups of organisms 172<br/>Resolving Conflicts Among Interest Groups<br/>Parties with a stake in the outcome of ESA decisions include conservationists; developers; other private and<br/>public industries; private individuals; academics; local, state, and federal agencies; tribes; and others. Public<br/>participation in ESA decisions is a part of the legislation and implementing regulations in the form of<br/>opportunities to petition for listing of species and to offer comments on proposed actions. Negotiated agreements<br/>among FWS and interested parties are a common part of ESA implementation through Section 7 provisions for<br/>consultation among federal agencies and Section 10 provisions for development of habitat conservation plans by<br/>private developers. In Section 7 consultations, FWS is charged with issuing an opinion on whether a proposed<br/>action by a federal agency is likely to jeopardize the continued existence of a listed species. In practice, the<br/>proposed actions are almost always negotiated informally, with comments by FWS and changes in plans by the<br/>developing agency, such that by the time the formal consultation occurs, jeopardy opinions are extremely rare<br/>(Houck, 1993). Although the results might be criticized, the process of negotiating agreement between federal<br/>agencies is entirely consistent with directives for constructive cooperation among federal agencies (Cleland,<br/>1991; Wondolleck et al., 1994).<br/>The process of developing habitat conservation plans under Section 10 (Chapter 4; Bean et al., 1991) is also<br/>a collaborative, negotiated process between FWS and individuals seeking the incidental take permit. Again, the<br/>results of this process often have been criticized.<br/>A combination of methods from decision analysis and dispute resolution can offer disputing parties a way<br/>out of the dilemma of how to combine the best scientific analysis with attention to the conflicting values that<br/>often are involved in making controversial decisions regarding endangered species. Dispute resolution brings<br/>multiple parties together to develop a plan that meets the essential needs of all (Fisher et al., 1991). Decision<br/>analysis can help facilitate this process by providing a structure for representing the values and the scientific<br/>beliefs that inform each party's positions (Maguire and Boiney, 1994). Decision analysis and dispute resolution<br/>direct attention to the objectives and underlying interests that a decision is supposed to reflect. The parts of<br/>decision analysis that focus on objectives, priorities, tradeoffs, and criteria can help with this values-structuring<br/>part of the analysis (Keeney, 1992). One of the tasks of a collaborative problem-solving process is disentangling<br/>disagreements about facts from differences in values. This process is difficult at best, and even harder when there<br/>is substantial scientific uncertainty, as is so often the case in ESA decisions. Decision analysis separates each<br/>party's view of the facts of the matter from that party's value structure, which is helpful in identifying what each<br/>party 173<br/>believes in terms of facts and values. Some of the benefits of decision analysis and dispute resolution can be<br/>realized with a qualitative analysis. In other cases, quantitative analysis, such as sensitivity analysis or value of<br/>information analysis, can help promote agreement by showing where additional information might help develop<br/>a consensus plan and how much additional information might be worth acquiring (Maguire and Boiney, 1994).<br/>Such procedures can help ensure that negotiations under Section 7 and Section 10 do a good job of incorporating<br/>both science and values.<br/>Implementing Structured Approaches in the Agencies<br/>A call for a more structured approach to ESA decisions using tools such as decision analysis is not<br/>necessarily a call for more extensive analysis or research, neither of which could be supported with current<br/>resources. Rather, it is a way of making better use of available information to address the problems at hand.<br/>Many of the benefits of structured analysis can be realized with a relatively quick and qualitative application of<br/>decision and risk concepts (Behn and Vaupel, 1982). Those concepts include explicitly identifying objectives,<br/>setting priorities among objectives, and establishing criteria for measuring progress toward those objectives;<br/>clearly weighing tradeoffs among conflicting objectives, whether these tradeoffs are forced by the language of<br/>the ESA or by limited resources for implementing it; and explicitly considering probabilistic information,<br/>making better use of expert opinion, and providing more coherent ways of combining data and opinion to<br/>estimate probabilities.
